records.content: In assignment 1, two of team members interested in different factors affect students’academic performance. Therefore, when choosing the topic, our team decided to collect four datasets from Kaggle in the purpose of discovering how different factors influence students’ academic performance.  Although each dataset contains different variables and was collected from different populations, they all focus on the relationship between students’ study time and their academic performance. This allows us to test the same topic with multiple perspectives and datasets as evidence. Based on the datasets, we developed two different arguments to compare how different student lifestyles (strict versus chill lifestyles) impact on academic performance. Each argument will be supported by several substantiations that investigate specific factors such as study time, stress level, parental education, social time, etc., and different leisure activities.

hierarchy.lvl3: Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance
Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance

records.content: The first dataset used in this project is about Student Lifestyle (cite), providing the information about how different factors in student lifestyle (such as students’ daily habits across study, extracurriculars, sleep, socializing, stress levels, CGPA, and physical activities) can influence their academic performance (represented by GPA) and student well-being. This dataset contains 2000 records and 8 variables. In this project, we decided to use variables such as study hours, social hours, physical activity, stress level, GPA. For data preprocessing, we first checked the dataset structure by using pandas.DataFrame.head (n=5) to preview the first five rows, basic descriptive statistics through the kaggle dataset column, then checking variable name, data types, and missing values. Variables that were not relevant to our research were deleted. Second, for clearer visualization and consistency between datasets with different grading systems (e.g., GPA, final grade), we encoded students grade variables into categorical variable called Grade Group. Instead of using fixed score thresholds, we used a percentile tertile grouping (pandas.qcut) to divide students grade into three approximately equal-sized groups: Low, Medium, High. This allows us to ensure comparable proportions of grading across datasets. Finally, instead of merging dataset, we used each dataset to answer specific substantiation within our broader argument. This approach allows us to build more coherent storyline and show consistent patterns, while avoiding incorrect comparison between datasets with different samples and measurement scales.

This project is about the impact of lifestyle on education.

What factors help improve academic performance has always been a crucial question of generations of students and also researchers in the education field (e.g. Adelantado-Renau et al. 2019, Singh et al., 2019, Camacho-Morles et al., 2021).

In some perspectives, a successful study method is investing most of study time per day without any distraction from social activities, gaming or entertainment. Previous study shows that study habits, skills, and attitudes can predict student academic performance, meaning that having serious learning behaviors for everyday plays an important role in grades (Credé & Kuncel, 2008).

However, nowadays, student life is not just studying. They also spent time socializing and participating in other physical or extracurricular activities. Studying in a strict way might not be the most effective method to acquire high scores. Barbosa et al. (2020) found that sports participation may benefit students’ academic achievement. Besides, according to Gilman et al.’s (2004) findings, participating in extracurricular activities in a structured way can also enhance school performance of students.

Therefore, this data story aims to investigate whether living chill or living strictly would be more beneficial to students’ study, and the role of external variables such as stress level and parental supervision in the relationship between type of lifestyle and academic performance. Four datasets shared on Kaggle were used to structure the story: “Student lifestyle dataset” (Kumar, 2025), “Gaming vs Academic Performance” (AIExplorer77., n.d.), “Student Alcohol Consumption” (Cortez & SIlva, 2008) and “Student Habits vs Academic Performance” (Jayaantanaath., n.d.).

Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance

records.content: Having a strict lifestyle, such as focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities, may improve students’ academic performance.

Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance

records.content:
Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run plots/study_time_plot.ipynb



Four datasets show the same patterns, in which students in the high grade group tend to spend more time studying than students in the medium and low grease group. In the dataset Student Alcohol Consumption, students who study Math will have higher study time than those who study Portuguese. Overall, even though the datasets come from different samples and use different grading systems, the consistent pattern indicates that academic performance is strongly correlated with higher academic performance.

hierarchy.lvl1: Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance
Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance

records.content:
Student with better academic discipline tend to perform better in academic performance.

%run plots/attendance_plot.ipynb



The dataset gaming and student habit, which uses attendance percentage, show that students in high grade groups tend to have slightly higher average attendance and more academic discipline. The alcohol consumption dataset, which uses the opposite measurement - absence level, shows that students who achieve higher grades consistently have fewest average absences, while those in low and medium grade groups tend to be absent more often. Moreover, students studying Math tend to be absent more than students studying Portuguese.

hierarchy.lvl1: Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance
Argument 1: Strict lifestyle and academic performance

records.content:
Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes

%run plots/extra_class_plot.ipynb



In this substantiation, the line chart was used to show the academic investment represented by paid extra classes. The visualization indicates that the relationship between extra classes and study time lead to higher grades of students. Students who combine intensive study time with additional classes often achieve higher grades. However, the pattern does not show the same regarding Math and Portuguese. When it comes to Math students, those who joined academic support classes tend to have gradually increased grades even if they have low or high study time. In contrast, Math students who do not attend extra classes will have lower grades if they spend less time studying, but higher grades when they invest enough time for studying. Therefore, even if math students attend extra classes or not, or dedicate time to intensive study each day, it does not represent their grades; it lies in their level of focus and comprehension (cite). In terms of Portuguese, students who spend more time studying without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them.  This difference lies in the nature of the subject. Mathematics requires teaching and various learning methods from the teacher (cite), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice will be more effective in improving students’ language skills and vocabulary (cite).

The balanced lifestyle is about combining studying and leisure activities.

%run plots/alcohol_consumption_plot.ipynb
